---
title: "Wasserstein Distance"
subtitle: "Optimal Transport: Minimizing the physical work required to reshape one distribution into another."
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
---

## 1. Definitions {#sec-definitions}

::: {.thm-box .thm-definition}
### Definition: Wasserstein Distance

For two probability distributions $P$ and $Q$, the $1^{st}$ Wasserstein Distance $W_1$ is defined as the infimum (minimum) expected cost of transporting mass from $P$ to $Q$:

$$
W_1(P, Q) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(P, Q)} \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \gamma} \big[ \|x - y\| \big]
$$

#### Breaking down the math:
1. **$\Pi(P, Q)$**: The set of all possible "transport plans". A joint distribution plan $\gamma(x, y)$ dictates exactly how much mass to move from location $x$ to location $y$. To be a valid plan, its marginals must equal $P$ and $Q$.
2. **$\|x - y\|$**: The physical distance between location $x$ and $y$ in the underlying metric space.
3. **$\mathbb{E}$**: The expected cost (work) of executing the transport plan.
4. **$\inf$**: The infimum. We are searching for the single *optimal* transport plan that requires the absolute least amount of work.
:::

---

## 2. Intuitions & Remarks {#sec-intuitions}

::: {.thm-box .thm-remark}
### The Earth Mover's Distance (EMD)

The Wasserstein Distance is intuitively known as the **Earth Mover's Distance**.

Imagine probability distributions as physical piles of dirt (Earth). The Wasserstein distance asks: *What is the absolute minimum amount of "work" required to physically shovel the dirt from distribution $P$ until it perfectly matches the shape of distribution $Q$?* 

Work in physics is defined as: `Amount of dirt moved` $\times$ `Distance moved`.

Because it explicitly measures the *physical distance* the mass must travel, the Wasserstein distance provides a smooth, meaningful value (and crucially, a non-zero gradient) even when the distributions are miles apart and do not overlap at all.
:::

---

## 3. Formulas & Key Properties {#sec-properties}

::: {.thm-box .thm-theorem}
### Overcoming the Limits of Divergences

Both the Kullback-Leibler (KL) and Jensen-Shannon (JSD) divergences are fundamentally based on the *ratio* of probabilities $P(x) / Q(x)$ at exactly the same spatial location $x$. 

This mathematical foundation creates a severe problem: if the two distributions have **disjoint supports** (meaning they don't overlap in space), these divergences either become infinite or saturate at a constant value. 

When divergences saturate, their gradients become **exactly zero**. In Machine Learning, if your loss function has zero gradient, your neural network receives no signal and cannot learn. 

Wasserstein distance measures the *horizontal cost* of moving the mass, rather than just the *vertical ratio* at a fixed location, completely bypassing this issue.
:::

---

## 4. Graphs & Visualizations {#sec-visualizations}

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    subgraph The Gradient Problem
        A[Distribution P] ---|No Overlap| B[Distribution Q]
        AB(KL Divergence: Infinity\nJS Divergence: log 2\nGradient: ZERO)
    end
    
    subgraph The Optimal Transport Solution
        C[Pile of Dirt P] -->|Move Mass * Distance| D[Hole Q]
        CD(Wasserstein Distance: D x M\nGradient: Constant Vector towards Q)
    end
    
    style A fill:#161b22,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#161b22,stroke:#f87171,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style AB fill:#161b22,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    
    style C fill:#161b22,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#161b22,stroke:#f87171,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style CD fill:#161b22,stroke:#00ff88,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---

## 5. Relations to Other Fields {#sec-relations}

| Field | Interpretation / Mapping | Application Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Generative AI** | Evaluating the distance between generated distributions and real data when they don't overlap. | Wasserstein GANs (WGAN) |
| **Operations Research** | Optimal assignment of resources from supply nodes to demand nodes. | Transport/Logistics optimization |
| **Computer Vision** | Comparing color histograms or image signatures in a way that respects the distance between colors. | Image Retrieval (Rubner et al.) |

::: {.thm-box .thm-example}
### Machine Learning Context: WGANs and Duality

The mathematical definition of $W_1(P, Q)$ is beautiful, but computationally intractable to calculate directly because searching over all possible joint distributions $\Pi(P, Q)$ is impossible for high-dimensional data like images.

In 2017, the landmark paper *Wasserstein GAN* solved this by applying the **Kantorovich-Rubinstein Duality**. The duality theorem states that instead of searching for the optimal transport plan, you can calculate the Wasserstein distance by finding a **1-Lipschitz continuous function** $f$ that maximizes the difference in expectations:

$$
W_1(P, Q) = \sup_{\|f\|_L \le 1} \left( \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P}[f(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{y \sim Q}[f(y)] \right)
$$

In a WGAN, the Discriminator (now called a "Critic") is simply a neural network parameterized to represent $f(x)$. To enforce the $1$-Lipschitz constraint ($\|f\|_L \le 1$), modern implementations use *Gradient Penalty* (WGAN-GP), ensuring the gradients of the critic respect a norm of $1$. WGANs completely eliminate the vanishing gradient problem, resulting in vastly more stable training.
:::

---

## 6. Interactive Simulation {#sec-simulation}

```{=html}
<div class="simulation-card" style="background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.03); border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1); border-radius: 16px; padding: 2rem; margin: 2rem 0; box-shadow: 0 8px 32px rgba(0,0,0,0.4); backdrop-filter: blur(12px);">
  <h4 style="margin-top: 0; color: #fff; font-family: 'Outfit', sans-serif;">Interactive Simulation: The Earth Mover</h4>
  <p style="color: #a0a2b6; font-size: 0.95rem; margin-bottom: 1.5rem;">
    Distribution P (blue) is a pile of dirt. Distribution Q (orange) is a hole of exactly the same volume. Drag the slider to move the hole Q away from the dirt P. Watch how the Earth Mover's Distance increases linearly with the physical separation, providing a constant, non-zero gradient even when they completely stop overlapping.
  </p>

  <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 1.5rem; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto;">
    <!-- SVG Chart -->
    <svg id="emd-chart" viewBox="0 0 500 250" style="width: 100%; height: auto; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.2); border-radius: 12px; border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.05);">
      <!-- Ground line -->
      <line x1="50" y1="150" x2="450" y2="150" stroke="#8b949e" stroke-width="2" />

      <!-- Distance Arrow -->
      <line id="emd-arrow" x1="150" y1="60" x2="250" y2="60" stroke="#00ff88" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrowhead)" marker-start="url(#arrowtail)" />
      <text id="emd-arrow-text" x="200" y="50" fill="#00ff88" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Outfit">Distance = 100m</text>

      <defs>
        <marker id="arrowhead" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="9" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
          <polygon points="0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7" fill="#00ff88" />
        </marker>
        <marker id="arrowtail" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="1" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
          <polygon points="10 0, 0 3.5, 10 7" fill="#00ff88" />
        </marker>
      </defs>

      <!-- Curve paths -->
      <!-- P (Dirt Pile) -->
      <path d="M 110 150 C 130 150, 130 80, 150 80 C 170 80, 170 150, 190 150 Z" fill="rgba(96, 165, 250, 0.6)" stroke="#60a5fa" stroke-width="2" />
      <text x="150" y="110" fill="#fff" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Outfit">P (Dirt)</text>

      <!-- Q (Hole) -->
      <path id="emd-q-path" d="M 210 150 C 230 150, 230 220, 250 220 C 270 220, 270 150, 290 150 Z" fill="rgba(248, 113, 113, 0.3)" stroke="#f87171" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4,4" />
      <text id="emd-q-text" x="250" y="190" fill="#f87171" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Outfit">Q (Hole)</text>
    </svg>

    <!-- Controls and Readout -->
    <div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr auto; gap: 1.5rem; align-items: center; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.15); padding: 1rem; border-radius: 12px; border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.03);">
      <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 0.5rem;">
        <label for="emd-slider" style="color: #c9d1d9; font-size: 0.85rem; font-weight: 600; font-family: 'Outfit';">Move Target Hole Q:</label>
        <input id="emd-slider" type="range" min="150" max="400" step="1" value="250" style="width: 100%; accent-color: #00ff88; cursor: pointer;" />
      </div>
      <div style="text-align: right; font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.9rem; line-height: 1.4; color: #fff;">
        <div style="color: #8b949e;">Mass (Volume) = 1.0</div>
        <div>Separation = <span id="emd-val-dist" style="color: #60a5fa; font-weight: 700;">100</span>m</div>
        <div style="margin-top: 5px; font-size: 1.1rem; color: #00ff88;">W₁ Cost = <span id="emd-val-cost" style="font-weight: 700;">100.0</span> work</div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <script>
    document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function() {
      const slider = document.getElementById("emd-slider");
      const valDist = document.getElementById("emd-val-dist");
      const valCost = document.getElementById("emd-val-cost");
      
      const pathQ = document.getElementById("emd-q-path");
      const textQ = document.getElementById("emd-q-text");
      
      const arrow = document.getElementById("emd-arrow");
      const textArrow = document.getElementById("emd-arrow-text");

      const pCenter = 150;

      function update() {
        const qCenter = parseFloat(slider.value);
        const distance = Math.abs(qCenter - pCenter);

        valDist.textContent = distance.toFixed(0);
        valCost.textContent = distance.toFixed(1);

        // Update Q path
        const dQ = `M ${qCenter - 40} 150 C ${qCenter - 20} 150, ${qCenter - 20} 220, ${qCenter} 220 C ${qCenter + 20} 220, ${qCenter + 20} 150, ${qCenter + 40} 150 Z`;
        pathQ.setAttribute("d", dQ);
        textQ.setAttribute("x", qCenter);

        // Update Arrow
        if (distance < 5) {
            arrow.style.opacity = 0;
            textArrow.style.opacity = 0;
        } else {
            arrow.style.opacity = 1;
            textArrow.style.opacity = 1;
            arrow.setAttribute("x2", qCenter);
            textArrow.setAttribute("x", (pCenter + qCenter) / 2);
            textArrow.textContent = `Distance = ${distance.toFixed(0)}m`;
        }
      }

      slider.addEventListener("input", update);
      update(); // init
    });
  </script>
</div>
```

---

## 7. Practice Problems {#sec-problems}

### Problem 1: Disjoint Supports and Gradients {#problem-1 .thm-box .thm-problem}

Explain mathematically why the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence provides a zero gradient when two distributions are completely disjoint, but the Wasserstein distance provides a highly useful constant gradient.
<details class="solution-toggle">
<summary>Reveal Solution to Problem 1</summary>
<div class="solution-body">

::: {.thm-box .thm-solution}
<div class="solution-heading">Solution</div>

Let $P$ be the true data distribution (a spike at $x=0$) and $Q_\theta$ be our parameterized model distribution (a spike at $x=\theta$). Suppose they are currently disjoint ($\theta > 0$).

**For KL Divergence:**
The divergence is defined by the point-wise ratio at exactly the same spatial location $x$:
$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q_\theta) = \sum P(x) \log \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q_\theta(x)} \right)
$$
Since $P$ is only non-zero at $x=0$, we only evaluate the ratio at $x=0$.
However, $Q_\theta(0) = 0$ because $Q_\theta$ is a spike at $\theta$.
Thus, $D_{KL} = 1 \cdot \log\left(\frac{1}{0}\right) = \infty$. 

Because it is $\infty$ for all $\theta > 0$, the derivative with respect to $\theta$ is exactly $0$. The loss landscape is a flat, infinite plateau, meaning the network has absolutely no idea which direction to move $\theta$ to find the true data.

**For Wasserstein Distance:**
The Earth Mover's distance measures the physical work to transport the spike of mass from $x=\theta$ to $x=0$. Since the total mass is $1$, the work is simply:
$$
W_1(P, Q_\theta) = 1 \cdot \|\theta - 0\| = |\theta|
$$
The gradient with respect to $\theta$ (for $\theta > 0$) is exactly $1$. 

The loss landscape is a perfect V-shape $f(\theta) = |\theta|$. This gives a beautiful, constant, non-zero gradient pointing directly toward the true data, allowing the neural network to effortlessly slide $\theta$ down to $0$ via gradient descent. $\blacksquare$
:::

</div>
</details>
